---
target_audience: B2B startup marketers revising pricing pages
viewer_problem: They keep polishing copy while buyers still compare only price
core_promise: Show the framing change that makes a higher tier feel justified
primary_takeaway: Anchors shape perceived value before feature details matter
thesis: Pricing pages that skip the comparison anchor miss the signal buyers use first
audience_belief_to_challenge: Buyers evaluate pricing tiers by counting features
core_mechanism: Anchoring changes perceived value before feature details are compared
lens: buyer psychology teardown
format: contrarian breakdown
tone: sharp but practical
cta: Audit your highest-priced plan against the decoy effect
why_this_version_should_exist: It turns abstract pricing theory into a concrete page review

proof_anchors:
---
proof_id: proof_01
summary: The first price seen creates a reference point that distorts all subsequent evaluation
source_ids: src_01, src_02
---
---
proof_id: proof_02
summary: The decoy effect is strongest when one tier makes the target tier feel more reasonable
source_ids: src_01, src_03
---

safe_claims:
---
claim_id: claim_01
claim: Anchoring changes perceived value when the higher-price option is presented first
supporting_proof_ids: proof_01, proof_02
---
---
claim_id: claim_02
claim: Buyers compare relative differences more than absolute feature counts
supporting_proof_ids: src_01
---

beat_claim_plan:
---
beat_id: beat_01
beat_name: Hook
goal: Make the pricing mistake feel obvious in retrospect
claim_ids: claim_01
proof_anchor_ids: proof_01
---
---
beat_id: beat_02
beat_name: Reframe
goal: Show the comparison anchor buyers actually use
claim_ids: claim_02
proof_anchor_ids: proof_02
---
---
beat_id: beat_03
beat_name: CTA
goal: Prompt one concrete change to the pricing card
claim_ids: claim_01
proof_anchor_ids:
---
---
target_audience: SaaS founders with discount-heavy sales cycles
viewer_problem: Discounts are masking weak positioning
core_promise: Explain how to tell when a pricing objection is really a packaging objection
primary_takeaway: Repeated discounting usually means the offer ladder is unclear
thesis: Repeated discounting usually signals an unclear offer ladder, not just a pricing problem
audience_belief_to_challenge: Discount requests are pricing objections that need a discount to resolve
core_mechanism: The ladder is unclear when buyers cannot see the value step-up between tiers
lens: sales pattern diagnosis
format: tactical explainer
tone: direct and diagnostic
cta: Pull three lost-call notes and tag the objection pattern
why_this_version_should_exist: It links pricing strategy to day-to-day deal review

proof_anchors:
---
proof_id: proof_03
summary: Repeated discounting correlates with unclear positioning, not just price sensitivity
source_ids: src_04
---

safe_claims:
---
claim_id: claim_03
claim: Discount requests often signal ladder ambiguity, not price sensitivity
supporting_proof_ids: proof_03
---

beat_claim_plan:
---
beat_id: beat_04
beat_name: Hook
goal: Surface the pattern of discounting as a positioning signal
claim_ids: claim_03
proof_anchor_ids: proof_03
---
---
beat_id: beat_05
beat_name: Reframe
goal: Show what the ladder looks like when it is clear
claim_ids: claim_03
proof_anchor_ids:
---
